comment.body:
Hi Alma, thank you for the wonderful writing today. It helped me a lot to understand the geo impact on the market

I'm curious though why you are calling for another big leg down as far as SPX 4000, even if negotiations are going well?
We're currently (potentially) in a short squeeze that seems to be also driven by some good geo news (trump / russia negotiation). If mid-May meetings all go well, then isn't it possible we just continue to ride up as the market gets news tariffs would be rolled back?

comment.body: I believe what she has hinted at a couple of times recently is that the downside may not be the big super low low after all. But that in light of any good news or positive developments that show US may gain the upper hand and get out of the woods after all the risks are still there for another drop just maybe not as low at risk of blowing up all their progress at getting some liquidity back in the market but enough of one that will make buying into things for those on the sidelines far more attractive as its with a lot of good things on the horizon as opposed to all out negativity. Sentiment is so one way right now the move up will alleviate some fears if its on the back of good news with regards to tariffs, peace in Ukraine talks, etc.

comment.body:
To my understanding if the big boys aren’t buying yet, we won’t go up a lot (talking about new ath). 
So the market will go down when there are no buyers. And when it goes down and the big money feel confident they will buy. Even when it goes down. Then news come out and they can start push the market up

comment.body:
Hi Alma, thanks for all the info and perspective, especially around the events of recent. Very helpful and gives clarity on how things are looking going forward.  I understand all the macro points (Russia, EU, China topics) but my one question is the other goal for Trump: defeating stagflation.  (I always remembered Trump had TWO GOALS: ceasefire and defeating stagflation :-))

I know you have repeatedly said in your posts that stagflation still persists (and that it will persist in the EU as well).  Are you believing the negative wealth effect will have run its course after all these events have passed? It seems like too short of a timeline. The April data coming out in May will really have to support this because based on what you said about FOMC/Powell being dovish in June, it seems to me there would need to a big shift in data that would support that stagflation no longer persists.  Can you share your thoughts about this?  Thank you!

(I always remembered Trump had TWO GOALS: ceasefire and defeating stagflation :-))

I know you have repeatedly said in your posts that stagflation still persists (and that it will persist in the EU as well).  Are you believing the negative wealth effect will have run its course after all these events have passed? It seems like too short of a timeline. The April data coming out in May will really have to support this because based on what you said about FOMC/Powell being dovish in June, it seems to me there would need to a big shift in data that would support that stagflation no longer persists.  Can you share your thoughts about this?  Thank you!

comment.body: My two cents: Alma's note this morning was written before knowing about Putin still meeting with Witkoff and that meeting going well, which in turn is a boost of clarity in the macro environment that a ceasefire is possible at this point.  That in turn means that other causal points for a further, deeper drop are *maybe* off the table.  Thats how I was interpreting it.

comment.body:
same here. But that might be coming from trying to use macro news to interpret day-day movement of the markets. I think there is a distinction between two cases here
1.) Trumps plan all works out and he gets what he wants and we keep onto our usual plan. This would mean likely a retest of the bottom and then consolidate around there. 
2.) Trumps plan doesn't work out. Then the low will easily get taken out.

comment.body: That means that the close-close return isn't compressing, buyers and sellers haven't "met" on a price. This causes volatility to expand. So the fact that RV is doing this and IV is getting sold likely just means that its coming from put selling on SPX. Vix term structure hasn't changed much relative tot his move and is still in backwardation.

comment.body:
Let’s take this one step at a time. First question I have is, what is the basis for thinking that these tariffs are simply about forcing a European supported peace deal with Russia/Ukraine? Tariffs have been a major issue for Trump for the last 30-40 years. If you listen to the Tim Dillon interview with Steve Bannon, the motivation seems to be genuine with wanting to make progress with tariffs and to genuinely increase domestic production that supports workers. Here’s the interview:

https://youtu.be/S9aXjWdGWH8?si=Nz_RT7eFPDiIDBWM

Maybe this is just added leverage, and he can make some concessions with the EU if they capitulate on Ukraine, but I highly doubt this would impact Chinese tariffs and I doubt every single one would be dropped.

Second question is, we are going through Q1 earnings season (Jan-March). Just a question for everyone… when were the tariffs enacted? April 2nd, Q2.

So we won’t see any impact on earnings hit for another 3-4 months. How does that play into all this?

Third question, the $9 trillion dollars of debt needs to be refinanced starting in H2 of this year.

Unless the economy roasts through the floor over the next 2 months and Powell drops rates to COVID levels, which it won’t because tariffs haven’t hit the earnings tape, how is that higher interest rate on all that refinanced debt going to effect liquidity and growth prospects moving forward?

Won’t these recession and deflation scares take another 3-6 months to fully play out? I just don’t understand how it’s all going to get wrapped up in 1-2 months.

Moreover, if Trump just cancels all the tariffs on a Russia cease fire deal, then that is going to infuriate the MAGA base and completely destroy his credibly and the ability for the fight to conduct a successful re election campaign.

Just some thoughts here and I’m not saying I’m right.

I’m simply brainstorming, so please don’t have an “autoimmune response” and start attacking me.

I’m just sharing thoughts here.

I’m just sharing thoughts here.
comment.raw_body:
Let’s take this one step at a time. First question I have is, what is the basis for thinking that these tariffs are simply about forcing a European supported peace deal with Russia/Ukraine? Tariffs have been a major issue for Trump for the last 30-40 years. If you listen to the Tim Dillon interview with Steve Bannon, the motivation seems to be genuine with wanting to make progress with tariffs and to genuinely increase domestic production that supports workers. Here’s the interview:

https://youtu.be/S9aXjWdGWH8?si=Nz_RT7eFPDiIDBWM

Maybe this is just added leverage, and he can make some concessions with the EU if they capitulate on Ukraine, but I highly doubt this would impact Chinese tariffs and I doubt every single one would be dropped.

Second question is, we are going through Q1 earnings season (Jan-March). Just a question for everyone… when were the tariffs enacted? April 2nd, Q2.

So we won’t see any impact on earnings hit for another 3-4 months. How does that play into all this?

Third question, the $9 trillion dollars of debt needs to be refinanced starting in H2 of this year.

Unless the economy roasts through the floor over the next 2 months and Powell drops rates to COVID levels, which it won’t because tariffs haven’t hit the earnings tape, how is that higher interest rate on all that refinanced debt going to effect liquidity and growth prospects moving forward?

Won’t these recession and deflation scares take another 3-6 months to fully play out? I just don’t understand how it’s all going to get wrapped up in 1-2 months.

Moreover, if Trump just cancels all the tariffs on a Russia cease fire deal, then that is going to infuriate the MAGA base and completely destroy his credibly and the ability for the fight to conduct a successful re election campaign.

Just some thoughts here and I’m not saying I’m right.

I’m simply brainstorming, so please don’t have an “autoimmune response” and start attacking me.

I’m just sharing thoughts here.

comment.body: Voice to text did me no favors here lol. Basically I agree there is a lot of other things going on but if the risk of the immediate term situation is so big a negative outcome only exacerbates existing problems. How that plays out months from now I’m sure will still be an issue regardless but how impactful on the market will likely be a result of what is happening now as well.

comment.body: Like you the timing around some of these bigger factors is certainly something I’d want to know more about but I think so much of the immediate risk weighs so heavily that it would impact those factors down the road as well I think we’re taking a kind of short term view of this whole thing.
comment.raw_body: Like you the timing around some of these bigger factors is certainly something I’d want to know more about but I think so much of the immediate risk weighs so heavily that it would impact those factors down the road as well I think we’re taking a kind of short term view of this whole thing.

comment.body:
1.) Its not entirely about the peace deal, just a pressure tactic. The tariffs are there to bring manufacturing back to some extent. I believe that is the ultimate goal. EU knows that if ceasfire talks go in Russia's favor then Russia will start to move onto the balkans (his grand dream to rebuild the USSR). With trump tarriffs on them there is added pressure to sort of "bend the knee"

2.) No effects if there is a practically a trade embargo, thats why im surprised there's been no coverage at the ports. Market generally believes this is a temporary bump in trumps grand plan since tarriffs have been known to not benefit anyone (any history book would tell you that) so for an entire administration to go about as that as their econoic policy is wild. If he lifts it then any bad earnings get written off as a "itll be better next time"

3.) Tarriffs will affect inflation and slow consumer spending. Its not about earnings, its about the consumer. If the debt can't be refinanced you end up in a debt spiral. I think this happened to japan post WW2?

comment.body: Also you never really know you're in a recession until after the fact. Most recessions are caused by tightening policies by the fed. Jerome powell has demonstrated (especially during covid) that he isn't late to the game. This gives the market a lot of confidence.
